item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ A minor mistake, collapsed for possible spoilers
- Or is it intentional?

I noticed that in this article the name is "Fritz Obermayer", but in Nobody in Their Right Mind it seems to be "Fritz Obermeyer"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never seen Nobody used in such a deep and forthright manner before. Have to imagine that's at least part of the reason why this team did well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Similarly to what others have said, the interplay in the logs is wonderful, and for that, I'm comfortable upvoting. The writing overall is as high quality as I would expect from this author.

However, I have to say, the majority of this drips with "I am an intermediate contest entry." Had I stumbled upon this as a wholly independent piece, I'm not sure that the bulk of the story has the legs to stand on by itself. So much of the mystery and pacing inherent to the plot feels as though it relies on the reader being familiar with the broader events of this team's entries, making it feel difficult to separate from the larger canon. Heck, it feels as though I'm reading a monologue in a play, where the narrator is taking time to explain the situation as it stands, before returning to the "real" action of the tales. I understand why this piece exists in the scope of the greater canon/contest, and the writing is strong, but its purpose and status as an SCP are questionable to me when viewed independently of that lens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
nice read, although, the dialogue is VERY on the nose.

a +1 nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if the apathy of the Foundation in this one is not really appealling to me, the dialogues and the bits of characterization added to Nobody and White Suit are enough for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Curse you, Croquembouche, for stealing the words from my mouth.

I don't think I have too much to say that Croq hasn't already said. The dialogue between Maliz and anyone absolutely shines, but at the end of the day they are right and this isn't an SCP. Simply posting it in a numbered slot does not make it so. And the attempt to jam it into an SCP format really hurts it - it leaves me with a lot of questions of "why didn't the Foundation do X in response?" Stuff like this:

A man in a white suit was spotted putting up identical leaflets in Boise's North End. Each leaflet bore anomalous text that cannot be perceived.

gets left on the table. It's nothing major, but it ends up being a death by a thousand cuts situation.

But, that being said - I love the interplay between Maliz and the Man in White and Nobody too much. The Man in White playing off of Maliz's naivety that the Foundation has purpose and isn't fascist? Stroke of genius.

I'm gonna novote here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

> stories about the man in the white suit
> hub is called the man in the white suit
> scp about the man in the white suit
> man in a black suit

This isn't an SCP. The anomaly, from the perspective of the Foundation, is poorly-defined if at all - "SCP-5353 is a anomalous humanoid" is all we get. The containment procedures don't really imply that 5353 is ever intended to be contained, in fact it almost feels like it's lampshading the fact that this isn't really an SCP. The format isn't justified. I actually think this would work much better as a PoI report, and all you'd need to cut is the SCP boilerplate.

I thought both of the incident logs were particularly compelling, and I really enjoyed both the white suit's monologue and the following counterattack by Nobody. Playing the two of them off of each other is an interesting take, and the swing from "huh, the white suit is trustworthy" to "uh oh, neither of them are trustworthy" was effective. I'm left doubting everything about either of them. The note at the end gives me the impression that the man in the white suit is what Nobody becomes after they're not Nobody anymore. If that's the case then they're both sort of meta-compulsion victims, and they're both as bad as each other.

+ show block
– hide block

However, I thought the content surrounding the incident logs wasn't that interesting, and served as a poor framing device. I wasn't able to find clear connections between the different events in the tables, so none of them meant anything to me, they were just filler to add space between the incidents. It's a shame that the best content of the article is buried in collapsibles.

It's also a little frustrating that this article feels like it's simply observing what's happening. The article feels too attached to the status quo, presenting the interaction between the white suit and Nobody as an immutable universal truth, when it could have been an opportunity for the Foundation to disrupt. The story is summarised, but it's not interacted with; the Foundation has no agency and the article feels passive as a result. Maliz is forced to play along to the white suit/Nobody's rules - not once but twice - and all she can do is ask a question every now and then. She "attempted to apprehend the anomaly" all by herself? What did she expect?

The formatting echoes my concerns. This is an SCP document, so I don't know why the Nobody theme is applied to it. The clearance level header is huge, the bottom of the logo touching the bottom of my laptop screen, and all that screen space is used to add nothing at all to the article.

Overall, it's a -1 from me, although there are gold nuggets in the collapsibles that are weighed down by everything else.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it normal that the man on the photography wears a standard black suit instead of a white one ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, good point - I had a feeling while writing that it'd been used somewhere else :p I think the Ad Astra canon uses it too.

Shall replace it with something else now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haven't read the whole thing yet, but you mention a Protocol Lethe-4. Idk if you know, but Project Lithe is something that appears in the "attempts to assassinate thought" SCP. There's nothing wrong with you using it, but for me it took me out of the experience because I associated it with an unrelated SCP.

Other than that it looks promising :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Several anomalous symbols were painted by associates of PoI-001

Intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven’t even read the hub, but this is badass. I’m always envious of authors who have good dialogue down pat, and you sure do. Strong +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tight during crit, tight now. Nobody's Winning FTW!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With thanks to my lovely teammates Rounderhouse and Captain Kirby, as well as to Grigori Karpin, for looking it over.

This is Part 4 of Nobody's Winning's "The Man in the White Suit" series for the Canon Renaissance Contest, a reinvention of The Man Who Wasn't There canon.
